main_result: Baseline reference used for all divergence calculations.
key_numeric_finding: 1071 persons; 25000 rows each in visit, condition, drug, measurement, observation, and procedure tables.
interpretation: V0 provides the structural and analytical baseline for comparison.

perturbation_type: Missing gender and birthDate for approximately 15% of patients
main_result: Missing demographic fields were preserved after OMOP transformation.
key_numeric_finding: 161 of 1071 persons had missing/unknown gender and missing/zero birth year; 15.03% demographic missingness.
interpretation: V1 produced measurable demographic drift in OMOP person-level attributes.

main_result: Duplicate source Encounter IDs did not create duplicate OMOP visit_occurrence_id values.
key_numeric_finding: OMOP visit_occurrence_id was regenerated as sequential unique integer IDs; original FHIR Encounter.id was not preserved in visit_occurrence.
interpretation: V2 source-level identity defects were hidden by transformation logic, creating a traceability limitation rather than visible row-count drift.

main_result: Source-level semantic heterogeneity increased, but standardized OMOP concept mapping remained unmapped.
key_numeric_finding: Unique condition_source_value increased from 247 in V0 to 412 in V3; 170 new source-value patterns appeared; 100% of condition_concept_id values remained 0.
interpretation: V3 perturbation was preserved in condition_source_value but not resolved into standardized OMOP condition concepts.

primary_test: Medication completeness and drug exposure drift
main_result: Total drug_exposure row count remained stable, but person-level and source-value completeness changed.
key_numeric_finding: Persons with drug exposure increased from 609 to 666; unique drug_source_value increased from 146 to 150; missing drug_source_value rows increased from 9687 to 9843.
interpretation: V4 did not appear as simple medication row loss, but produced distributional drift in drug source-value completeness and person-level exposure assignment.
